subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3. In this unit you will study two of the biggest ideas in all of science: forces and energy. A force is a push or a pull. Energy is the thing that lets change happen. Together, these two ideas explain almost everything that moves, falls, floats, warms up, lights up or comes to a stop around you, from the camel that kneels down at your homestead to the Moon that hangs over the Indian Ocean at night.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.
Describe gravity as a non-contact force of attraction that exists around every object that has mass, and explain that it acts towards the centre of a planet or star.
Distinguish clearly between mass and weight, giving the correct unit for each (kilograms for mass, newtons for weight).
Calculate weight from mass using the relationship weight = mass x gravitational field strength, showing every step of your working.
Explain why your weight changes on the Moon and on other planets while your mass stays the same.
Describe the Solar System: the Sun at the centre, the eight planets and their orbits, and how a scientific model helps us picture what we cannot see directly.
Explain movement in space: why planets orbit the Sun, why the Earth spinning on its axis gives us day and night, and why there is no air resistance in the vacuum of space.
Explain the tides as the daily rise and fall of the ocean caused mainly by the gravitational pull of the Moon, and describe spring tides and neap tides.
Identify the main forms of energy (kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic, chemical, thermal, light, sound and electrical) and give an everyday example of each.
Describe energy transfers, using the idea that energy is stored, then transferred from one store to another, and that some energy is always dissipated (wasted).

concepts.title: Assalaamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3. In this unit you…
concepts.explanation: Assalaamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3. In this unit you will study two of the biggest ideas in all of science: forces and energy. A force is a push or a pull. Energy is the thing that lets change happen. Together, these two ideas explain almost everything that moves, falls, floats, warms up, lights up or comes to a stop around you, from the camel that kneels down at your homestead to the Moon that hangs over the Indian Ocean at night.

concepts.title: Think about your own day. When you lift a jerrican of water…
concepts.explanation: Think about your own day. When you lift a jerrican of water from the well, you feel your arms working against something that pulls the water downward. That pull is gravity. When you watch the tide creep up the shore at Kismaayo or Mombasa and then slide back out hours later, you are watching gravity from the Moon at work on a whole ocean. When you cook bariis over a charcoal fire, chemical energy stored in the charcoal becomes heat and light. When a solar panel on a clinic roof in Garissa charges a battery, light…
concepts.example: This unit is also about the sky above you. In the clear savanna night, far from city lights, you can see thousands of stars, the band of the Milky Way, and the bright planets. People in East Africa have watched this sky…

concepts.title: This unit is also about the sky above you. In the clear…
concepts.explanation: This unit is also about the sky above you. In the clear savanna night, far from city lights, you can see thousands of stars, the band of the Milky Way, and the bright planets. People in East Africa have watched this sky for thousands of years to know the seasons, to find their direction when travelling, and to sight the new crescent Moon that begins each month of the Hijri calendar. You will learn what the Solar System really is, why the planets move the way they do, why we have day and night, and why the Moon…
concepts.example: You are learning this at home, at your own pace, with your AI tutor ready to help you any time of the day or night, and with your teacher meeting you about twice a week. That means you are in charge of your own…

concepts.title: You are learning this at home, at your own pace, with your…
concepts.explanation: You are learning this at home, at your own pace, with your AI tutor ready to help you any time of the day or night, and with your teacher meeting you about twice a week. That means you are in charge of your own understanding. Read slowly, try every worked example with a pencil in your hand, do the small household investigations, and ask your AI tutor whenever something is not clear. Science is not a set of facts to swallow; it is a way of looking carefully at the world and asking good questions. Let us begin.
concepts.example: [Fact] Did You Know? The word 'gravity' comes from a Latin word meaning 'heavy'. But gravity is not only about heavy things falling. The very same force that makes a date fall from a palm tree also holds the Earth in…

concepts.title: [Fact] Did You Know? The word 'gravity' comes from a Latin…
concepts.explanation: [Fact] Did You Know? The word 'gravity' comes from a Latin word meaning 'heavy'. But gravity is not only about heavy things falling. The very same force that makes a date fall from a palm tree also holds the Earth in its yearly path around the Sun, keeps the Moon circling the Earth, and pulls the oceans into the daily tides. One idea, working everywhere in the universe at once.
concepts.example: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.

explorations.title: Build a Rubber-Band Forcemeter
explorations.context: To build a simple forcemeter (a device that measures force) from a rubber band, and to use it to compare the weights of different household objects.
explorations.prompt: Before you begin, write down what you think: Will a heavier object stretch the rubber band more or less than a lighter object? Why? Predict, then test.
explorations.answer: Which object stretched the band the most? What does that tell you about its weight compared with the others? Put your objects in order from lightest to heaviest, using only your stretch measurements. The rubber band stores elastic energy when it is stretched. Where does that energy come from when…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a simple forcemeter (a device that measures force) from a rubber band, and to use it to compare the weights of different household objects.

explorations.title: The Swinging Pendulum
explorations.context: To build a pendulum and investigate what affects how long it takes to swing back and forth, watching gravity and energy at work.
explorations.prompt: Predict: Will a longer string make the pendulum swing faster or slower? Will a heavier weight change the swing time? Write your predictions before testing.
explorations.answer: Did shortening the string make the pendulum swing more times or fewer times in 30 seconds? So did it swing faster or slower? Did making the bob heavier change the number of swings very much? What does this tell you about how gravity pulls on light and heavy objects? At the top of each swing the bob…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a pendulum and investigate what affects how long it takes to swing back and forth, watching gravity and energy at work.

explorations.title: Falling Objects and Air Resistance
explorations.context: To observe how gravity pulls objects down and how air resistance can change the way things fall.
explorations.prompt: Predict: If you drop a flat sheet of paper and a crumpled ball of the same paper at the same time, which lands first, and why?
explorations.answer: The flat sheet and the crumpled ball have the same mass (they are the same paper). Why does the crumpled ball land first? What force pulls both papers downward? What force slows the flat sheet more than the ball? In the vacuum of space or on the Moon, there is no air. Predict what would happen if…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe how gravity pulls objects down and how air resistance can change the way things fall.

explorations.title: Modelling the Solar System and Day/Night
explorations.context: To build a simple model of the Sun, Earth and Moon, and to use it to show why we have day and night and how the Moon orbits the Earth.
explorations.prompt: Predict: If a lamp is the Sun and a ball is the Earth, what will you see on the ball as you slowly turn it? Where is it 'daytime' and where is it 'night'?
explorations.answer: As you spin the ball, why does your marked town move from day into night and back again? What does the spinning represent in real life? Roughly how long does the real Earth take to spin around once? How long does one full orbit of the Sun take? When the pebble-Moon is on the far side of the ball…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a simple model of the Sun, Earth and Moon, and to use it to show why we have day and night and how the Moon orbits the Earth.

visualModels.purpose: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.

methods.title: Build a Rubber-Band Forcemeter
methods.example: To build a simple forcemeter (a device that measures force) from a rubber band, and to use it to compare the weights of different household objects.
steps: Hang the rubber band from the nail or hook so it dangles freely.
Attach the empty bag or cup to the bottom of the band.
Hold the ruler next to the band and record the position of the bottom of the empty bag. This is your starting mark (zero load).
Place one object in the bag. Wait for it to stop moving, then record the new position of the bottom of the bag.
Work out how far the band stretched by subtracting the starting mark from the new position.

methods.title: The Swinging Pendulum
methods.example: To build a pendulum and investigate what affects how long it takes to swing back and forth, watching gravity and energy at work.
steps: Tie the bob securely to one end of the string.
Tie or hold the other end so the bob hangs freely and can swing without hitting anything.
Pull the bob gently to one side (a small angle) and release it without pushing.
Count how many complete swings (over and back) happen in 30 seconds. Record it.
Repeat twice more and take the average, to make your result more reliable.

methods.title: Falling Objects and Air Resistance
methods.example: To observe how gravity pulls objects down and how air resistance can change the way things fall.
steps: Hold one flat sheet of paper in each hand at the same height. Drop both at once and watch. (They should fall similarly.)
Now crumple one sheet into a tight ball. Hold the flat sheet and the crumpled ball at the same height.
Drop both at the same moment and carefully watch which lands first.
Repeat two or three times so you are sure of what you see.
Try the same idea with the stone and the feather or leaf.

methods.title: Modelling the Solar System and Day/Night
methods.example: To build a simple model of the Sun, Earth and Moon, and to use it to show why we have day and night and how the Moon orbits the Earth.
steps: Darken the room and place the lamp on a table as the Sun.
Mark a spot on the ball to be your town, for example Mogadishu.
Hold the ball at arm's length so the lamp lights one side of it. Notice that only the half facing the lamp is lit; the other half is in shadow.
Slowly rotate the ball on the spot (spinning it on its axis) and watch your marked town move from the lit side (day) into the shadow side (night) and back again.
Now walk the ball slowly all the way around the lamp to show the Earth's yearly orbit.

workedExamples.title: 1: The weight of a sack of rice on Earth.
workedExamples.prompt: Amina buys a sack of bariis (rice) with a mass of 25 kg. What is its weight on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg?
workedExamples.solution: Given: mass m = 25 kg; gravitational field strength g = 10 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate: W = 25 x 10 = 250. Answer: The weight of the sack is 250 N on Earth.

workedExamples.title: 2: Your own weight.
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan has a mass of 40 kg. Calculate his weight on Earth (g = 10 N/kg).
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 40 kg; g = 10 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate: W = 40 x 10 = 400. Answer: Hassan weighs 400 N on Earth.

workedExamples.title: 3: The same person on the Moon.
workedExamples.prompt: The Moon's gravitational field strength is only about 1.6 N/kg, roughly one sixth of Earth's. What would Hassan weigh on the Moon, and what is his mass there?
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 40 kg (mass does not change); g on the Moon = 1.6 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate: W = 40 x 1.6 = 64. Answer: On the Moon Hassan would weigh only 64 N, but his mass is still 40 kg.

workedExamples.title: 4: Working backwards to find mass.
workedExamples.prompt: A goat is weighed on Earth and its weight is found to be 300 N. What is its mass? (g = 10 N/kg.)
workedExamples.solution: Given: W = 300 N; g = 10 N/kg; we must find m. Formula: Start from W = m x g, then rearrange to m = W / g. Calculate: m = 300 / 10 = 30. Answer: The goat's mass is 30 kg.

workedExamples.title: 5: A camel's weight.
workedExamples.prompt: A young camel has a mass of 220 kg. What is its weight on Earth (g = 10 N/kg)?
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 220 kg; g = 10 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate: W = 220 x 10 = 2200. Answer: The camel weighs 2200 N on Earth.

workedExamples.title: 6: Comparing two planets.
workedExamples.prompt: A rover has a mass of 180 kg. Calculate its weight on Earth (g = 10 N/kg) and on Mars (g = 3.7 N/kg), and describe what you notice.
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 180 kg; g(Earth) = 10 N/kg; g(Mars) = 3.7 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate on Earth: W = 180 x 10 = 1800 N. Calculate on Mars: W = 180 x 3.7 = 666 N.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: On the Moon, compared with Earth, an astronaut's weight is: (a) greater, (b) less, (c) the same, (d) zero.
workedExamples.solution: 7 (b) less The Moon's gravity is weaker, so weight is less even though mass is unchanged.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The object at the centre of our Solar System is: (a) the Earth, (b) the Moon, (c) the Sun, (d) Jupiter.
workedExamples.solution: 8 (c) the Sun The Sun sits at the centre and its gravity holds the planets in orbit.

practice.prompt: Gravity is best described as: (a) a contact force, (b) a non-contact force of attraction, (c) a push, (d) a form of energy.
practice.answer: 1 (b) a non-contact force of attraction Gravity acts across a gap, with no touching, and always pulls objects together.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The unit of mass is: (a) newton, (b) joule, (c) kilogram, (d) watt.
practice.answer: 2 (c) kilogram Mass is the quantity of matter, measured in kilograms.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The unit of weight is: (a) newton, (b) kilogram, (c) joule, (d) metre.
practice.answer: 3 (a) newton Weight is a force, and all forces are measured in newtons.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Earth's gravity pulls objects towards: (a) the sky, (b) the nearest mountain, (c) the centre of the Earth, (d) the Moon.
practice.answer: 4 (c) the centre of the Earth Earth's gravity always pulls towards its centre, which is why 'down' points there everywhere.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A bag has a mass of 10 kg. Its weight on Earth (g = 10 N/kg) is: (a) 1 N, (b) 10 N, (c) 100 N, (d) 1000 N.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: On the Moon, compared with Earth, an astronaut's mass is: (a) greater, (b) less, (c) the same, (d) zero.
practice.answer: 6 (c) the same Mass never changes with location; the astronaut is made of the same matter.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: On the Moon, compared with Earth, an astronaut's weight is: (a) greater, (b) less, (c) the same, (d) zero.
practice.answer: 7 (b) less The Moon's gravity is weaker, so weight is less even though mass is unchanged.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The object at the centre of our Solar System is: (a) the Earth, (b) the Moon, (c) the Sun, (d) Jupiter.
practice.answer: 8 (c) the Sun The Sun sits at the centre and its gravity holds the planets in orbit.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The curved path of a planet around the Sun is called its: (a) axis, (b) orbit, (c) tide, (d) nebula.
practice.answer: 9 (b) orbit An orbit is the curved path of one body around another in space.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Day and night are caused by: (a) the Sun moving round the Earth, (b) the Earth spinning on its axis, (c) clouds covering the Sun, (d) the tides.
practice.answer: 10 (b) the Earth spinning on its axis As the Earth turns, each place moves into and out of sunlight.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The tides are caused mainly by the gravity of: (a) the wind, (b) the Sun only, (c) the Moon, (d) the Earth's core.
practice.answer: 11 (c) the Moon The Moon's gravity is the main cause of tides; the Sun adds a smaller effect.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Energy stored in a stretched rubber band is: (a) chemical, (b) elastic, (c) thermal, (d) sound.
practice.answer: 12 (b) elastic Stretching or squashing an object stores elastic energy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Build a Rubber-Band Forcemeter
activities.materials: One thick rubber band (or a length of thin elastic).; A small cloth bag, a light plastic cup, or a small tin with a handle, to hold objects.; A ruler or a strip of paper marked in centimetres.; A nail, hook, or door handle to hang the band from.; Several small objects to weigh: a spoon, a stone, a…
steps: Hang the rubber band from the nail or hook so it dangles freely.
Attach the empty bag or cup to the bottom of the band.
Hold the ruler next to the band and record the position of the bottom of the empty bag. This is your starting mark (zero load).
Place one object in the bag. Wait for it to stop moving, then record the new position of the bottom of the bag.
Work out how far the band stretched by subtracting the starting mark from the new position.

activities.title: The Swinging Pendulum
activities.materials: A length of string or strong thread (about one metre).; A small heavy object to act as the bob: a metal nut, a small stone in a cloth, or a key.; A fixed point to hang from: a door frame, a nail, or a stick held over the edge of a table.; A clock, watch or phone that can count seconds.
steps: Tie the bob securely to one end of the string.
Tie or hold the other end so the bob hangs freely and can swing without hitting anything.
Pull the bob gently to one side (a small angle) and release it without pushing.
Count how many complete swings (over and back) happen in 30 seconds. Record it.
Repeat twice more and take the average, to make your result more reliable.

activities.title: Falling Objects and Air Resistance
activities.materials: Two identical sheets of paper.; A small stone and a feather or a dry leaf.; A safe, clear place to drop things from the same height (standing height is enough).
steps: Hold one flat sheet of paper in each hand at the same height. Drop both at once and watch. (They should fall similarly.)
Now crumple one sheet into a tight ball. Hold the flat sheet and the crumpled ball at the same height.
Drop both at the same moment and carefully watch which lands first.
Repeat two or three times so you are sure of what you see.
Try the same idea with the stone and the feather or leaf.

activities.title: Modelling the Solar System and Day/Night
activities.materials: A torch, lamp, or candle to act as the Sun (with adult help if using a flame).; A ball or a round fruit such as an orange to act as the Earth.; A small pebble or a smaller fruit to act as the Moon.; A small sticker or a spot of ink to mark 'your town' on the Earth-ball.; A darkened room.
steps: Darken the room and place the lamp on a table as the Sun.
Mark a spot on the ball to be your town, for example Mogadishu.
Hold the ball at arm's length so the lamp lights one side of it. Notice that only the half facing the lamp is lit; the other half is in shadow.
Slowly rotate the ball on the spot (spinning it on its axis) and watch your marked town move from the lit side (day) into the shadow side (night) and back again.
Now walk the ball slowly all the way around the lamp to show the Earth's yearly orbit.

terms: The quantity of matter in an object, measured in kilograms (kg); never changes with location.
The force of gravity on an object, measured in newtons (N); changes if gravity changes.
The force of gravity per kilogram; about 10 N/kg on Earth, 1.6 N/kg on the Moon.
The Sun and everything held by its gravity: the eight planets, their moons, and more.
The curved path of one object around another, held by gravity.
An imaginary line through a planet about which it spins.
A region with no particles, such as the space between the planets.
commonMistakes: Kilograms measure mass, not force. Weight is a force.
Saying mass changes on the Moon
Mass is the amount of matter, which does not change with location.
Thinking there is no gravity in space
Gravity fills space; it holds planets and moons in orbit.
Saying heavier things always fall faster
This is only true because of air resistance.
Saying energy is 'used up' and lost

realProblems.prompt: Amina's sack of maize has a mass of 45 kg. Calculate its weight on Earth (g = 10 N/kg). Show all four steps.
realProblems.answer: Given: m = 45 kg, g = 10 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate: W = 45 x 10 = 450. Answer: 450 N.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Given: m = 45 kg, g = 10 N/kg. Formula: W = m x g. Calculate: W = 45 x 10 = 450. Answer: 450 N.

realProblems.prompt: A camel has a mass of 400 kg. Calculate its weight on Earth. Then calculate its weight on the Moon, where g = 1.6 N/kg. Comment on what changed and what did not.
realProblems.answer: On Earth: W = 400 x 10 = 4000 N. On the Moon: W = 400 x 1.6 = 640 N. The weight changed (from 4000 N to 640 N) because the Moon's gravity is weaker, but the mass stayed 400 kg because the amount of matter did not change.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: On Earth: W = 400 x 10 = 4000 N. On the Moon: W = 400 x 1.6 = 640 N. The weight changed (from 4000 N to 640 N) because the Moon's gravity is weaker, but the mass stayed 400 kg because the amount of matter did not change.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman at Kismaayo notices the sea is very high at the new Moon. Explain why the tide is especially large at this time.
realProblems.answer: At new Moon, the Sun and the Moon are lined up on the same side (or opposite sides) of the Earth, so their gravitational pulls combine. This produces an especially large spring tide, with a very high high tide and a very low low tide.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: At new Moon, the Sun and the Moon are lined up on the same side (or opposite sides) of the Earth, so their gravitational pulls combine. This produces an especially large spring tide, with a very high high tide and a very low low tide.

realProblems.prompt: A family in Garissa uses a solar panel to charge a battery, which at night powers a light. Describe the energy transfers, from sunlight to the glowing bulb, naming each form of energy.
realProblems.answer: Sunlight (light energy) hits the solar panel and is transferred to electrical energy. The battery stores it as chemical energy. At night the battery transfers electrical energy to the bulb, which gives out light energy (and some wasted thermal energy).
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Sunlight (light energy) hits the solar panel and is transferred to electrical energy. The battery stores it as chemical energy. At night the battery transfers electrical energy to the bulb, which gives out light energy (and some wasted thermal energy).

realProblems.prompt: A water container weighing 200 N is lifted from the ground onto a shelf. What is its mass (g = 10 N/kg)? What form of energy does it gain by being lifted up?
realProblems.answer: Mass: rearrange W = m x g to m = W / g = 200 / 10 = 20 kg. By being lifted up, the container gains gravitational potential energy.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mass: rearrange W = m x g to m = W / g = 200 / 10 = 20 kg. By being lifted up, the container gains gravitational potential energy.

realProblems.prompt: A rover has a weight of 720 N on Earth. Without a calculator, work out its mass, then predict roughly its weight on Mars (g = 3.7 N/kg). Explain your reasoning.
realProblems.answer: Mass = W / g = 720 / 10 = 72 kg. On Mars, W = 72 x 3.7 = about 266 N. Reasoning: mass is unchanged at 72 kg; Mars's gravity (3.7 N/kg) is weaker than Earth's, so the weight is much less.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mass = W / g = 720 / 10 = 72 kg. On Mars, W = 72 x 3.7 = about 266 N. Reasoning: mass is unchanged at 72 kg; Mars's gravity (3.7 N/kg) is weaker than Earth's, so the weight is much less.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between mass and weight. Give the unit of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Mass is the quantity of matter in an object, measured in kilograms (kg), and it never changes with location. Weight is the force of gravity acting on that mass, measured in newtons (N), and it changes if gravity changes.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the relationship (formula) that connects weight, mass and gravitational field strength.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Weight = mass x gravitational field strength, or W = m x g. Weight is in N, mass in kg, and g in N/kg.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does your weight change on the Moon but your mass stay the same?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Your mass is the amount of matter you are made of, which does not change when you travel. Your weight depends on how strongly gravity pulls, and the Moon's gravity is weaker than Earth's, so you weigh less there while your mass stays the same.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the force that holds the planets in their orbits around the Sun.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The force of gravity, coming from the Sun, holds the planets in their orbits.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, using the idea of the Earth's axis, why we have day and night.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The Earth spins on its axis roughly once every 24 hours. The side facing the Sun has day; the side turned away is in shadow and has night. As the Earth turns, each place passes from day into night and back.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What causes the tides, and why are there very large tides at new Moon and full Moon?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The tides are caused mainly by the gravitational pull of the Moon (with a smaller pull from the Sun) drawing the ocean water into bulges. At new Moon and full Moon the Sun and Moon line up, so their pulls combine to give especially large spring tides.

questions.question: Gravity is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a non-contact force of attraction Gravity acts across a gap, with no touching, and always pulls objects together.

questions.question: The unit of mass is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: kilogram Mass is the quantity of matter, measured in kilograms.

questions.question: The unit of weight is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: newton Weight is a force, and all forces are measured in newtons.

questions.question: Earth's gravity pulls objects towards:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the centre of the Earth Earth's gravity always pulls towards its centre, which is why 'down' points there everywhere.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.

questions.question: On the Moon, compared with Earth, an astronaut's mass is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the same Mass never changes with location; the astronaut is made of the same matter.

questions.question: On the Moon, compared with Earth, an astronaut's weight is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: less The Moon's gravity is weaker, so weight is less even though mass is unchanged.

questions.question: The object at the centre of our Solar System is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Sun The Sun sits at the centre and its gravity holds the planets in orbit.

questions.question: The curved path of a planet around the Sun is called its:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: orbit An orbit is the curved path of one body around another in space.

options: the Sun moving round the Earth,
the Earth spinning on its axis,

questions.explanation: the Earth spinning on its axis As the Earth turns, each place moves into and out of sunlight.

questions.question: The tides are caused mainly by the gravity of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Moon The Moon's gravity is the main cause of tides; the Sun adds a smaller effect.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.
I can describe gravity as a non-contact force of attraction that exists around every object that has mass, and explain that it acts towards the centre of a planet or star.
I can distinguish clearly between mass and weight, giving the correct unit for each (kilograms for mass, newtons for weight).
I can calculate weight from mass using the relationship weight = mass x gravitational field strength, showing every step of your working.
I can explain why your weight changes on the Moon and on other planets while your mass stays the same.
I can describe the Solar System: the Sun at the centre, the eight planets and their orbits, and how a scientific model helps us picture what we cannot see directly.
I can explain movement in space: why planets orbit the Sun, why the Earth spinning on its axis gives us day and night, and why there is no air resistance in the vacuum of space.
I can explain the tides as the daily rise and fall of the ocean caused mainly by the gravitational pull of the Moon, and describe spring tides and neap tides.
